videos.title: Epic Highs & Unwelcome Lows I Had Cruising the Nile!
videos.description:
I reveal the best and very worst things I experienced on my AmaWaterways Nile River Cruise and time in Cairo, and answer if all things considered is it worth taking this trip. It's complicated

For bonus & exclusive content (including videos, articles, eBooks and more) become a channel member or patron:
---------------------------------------------------

ABOUT TIPS FOR TRAVELLERS:  If you want to get cruising right, Tips For Travellers makes it easy to plan and have the incredible cruise experiences you dream of every time. All my Tips For Travellers advice, tips and tricks tips draw on everything that I have learnt on the 101 cruises I have done to date.

#cruisetips #CruisingTips #cruisetipsandtricks

tags: nile river cruise luxor to aswan
videos.transcript: I never thought I would have to sleep on the bathroom floor on my AmaWaterways Nile River cruise to avoid a downside that no one warned me about. I instead had been told to watch out for safety issues, pushy vendors, and getting the infamous Egyptian upset stomach. So, how did I find it all? Was it worth it, even with those two nights of sleeping on the bathroom floor? Welcome aboard. I'm Gary Bembridge. Let’s start with the highs. First off, although I knew it would be impressive, nothing had prepared me for the actual grandeur of the sights. My AmaWaterways trip was 11 days, starting with 3-nights in Cairo, a 7-night Nile River Cruise sailing from Luxor to Aswan and back and one final night back in Cairo. While in the city they took us to see the three enormous pyramids and Great Sphinx at Giza, where many guests went on camel rides. Two other Cairo highlights for me were the Alabaster Mosque at the Citadel of Salah Eldin, and the Museum of Egyptian Antiquities. But for me, the best sights were on the cruise itself. At the start in Luxor, we visited the Valley of the Queens to see the remarkable Nefertari’s tomb, and others like Titi’s Tomb, wife of Rameses III. In the Valley of the Kings, I saw Tutankhamen's tomb, and three other magnificent tombs with the most remarkable being Titi’s husband Ramses III’s tomb stretching deep into the hillside. We also went spent time at the multi-level Queen Hatshepsut's temple carved into the mountain side. This is one of my favourites from the entire trip. Also in Luxor, we explored the 250-acre Karnak Temple complex, which transported me into scenes I remembered from Agatha Christie’s “Death on the Nile” film. We also ventured north to the Temple of Hathor, Goddess of Love, near Qena with its magnificent blue ceilings depicting the zodiac. Then on the sailings to and from Luxor to Aswan we called in at Edfu to see the enormous Temple of Horus, and at Kom Ombo visited the only temple dedicated to two gods, Sobek (the Crocodile God) and Haroeris (the Falcon-headed Good Doctor God). Once in Aswan we explored the Philae Temple, which was moved in its entirely from an island that was going to disappear under rising lake waters to a new island. I also experienced two things in Aswan that will stay with me for years. First, was sailing on a felucca from the AmaDahlia ship to have afternoon tea at the famous Old Cataract Hotel, where Agatha Christie spent time writing “Death on the Nile”. And second was Abu Simbel. These massive and jaw-dropping temples were moved 62 metres up and 200 metres along to avoid the rising waters when Lake Nasser was built in the 1960s. Ever since I was 10 years old, when I learnt of this, I wanted to see it. It was the highlight of the entire trip. Seeing so much packed into a 10-day trip was something I loved about this trip. Though with so many temples and tombs many have asked if I got “tomb fatigue”. While seeing so much could have got repetitive, it was not thanks to the Egyptologist Guide. On AmaWaterways, there was one per group of 24 passengers. To become an Egyptologist takes four years study, and I found mine, Nermeen a mountain of information. She was entertaining, knowledgeable and a dab hand at helping us navigate some of the challenges I come to later. I came away convinced that Nermeen was crucial in making this trip. So, if you ever cruise the Nile make sure you check how good the company you are considering is rated for the Egyptologist guides. Another best thing was sailing the Nile itself. Unfortunately, there wasn’t as much sailing as I'd hoped because the distance covered is not that far. It’s just 140 miles (225km) from Luxor to Aswan and many days we did not sail at all as we were docked overnight. More on that later as that brought some unwelcome downsides. But when sailing, it was beautiful and calming. I especially enjoyed our first day of sailing, when all the ships departed Luxor roughly the same time and raced each other down to the Esna locks between Luxor and Aswan. Only 2 ships can enter at a time, and it’s on a first come, first served basis. Watching the ships jostling down the river was very entertaining. One of the fun things once at the locks were the vendors in small rowboats that surrounded the ship, shouting up about what they're selling, throwing items up to guests high up on the sun deck and negotiating. It was entertaining and unthreatening, unlike the vendors on land that I will come to a later. Another thing I liked was my AmaWaterways fare, as with most Nile cruises, was all-inclusive, and pretty much everything was included, and included someone on hand guiding us all the time from arrival to departure, which as you will see later is very important. It was not a cheap trip and the 11-nights including pre- and post-stays in Cairo cost based on cabin type around $9,000 - $12,000 per couple excluding flights there. (That’s around £7,000 - £9,500) Within that fare, I liked that I was met before immigration at the airport and guided through, had all transfers, hotels in Cairo, flights to and from Luxor, the river cruise, entry to the sites, food (breakfast, lunch, afternoon tea and dinner), Wi-Fi (though not that good!), daily Sip & Sail hour with all drinks using Egyptian spirits every evening, Egyptian beers and wines with lunch and dinner, entertainment events, and the Cruise Manager (Walaa) and the Egyptologists. The only extra costs were optional and were gratuities (around $250 per person for the land and cruise teams), laundry and three optional tours (Abu Simbel at $350 as involved flights, Old Cataract Hotel Afternoon Tea at $50 and Camel rides at Giza). Those were the best parts, now let me talk about some unexpected lows about Nile River cruising, starting with this one. I was told by the crew that there are at least 250 ships sailing the Nile, which means it is very busy as all are sailing that short 120 Miles between Luxor and Aswan, which is why I saw 50 or more ships docked in some places. For example, as we pulled into Edfu to visit the Temple of Horus, I counted 50 ships there. And counted even more in Aswan. As there are limited places to dock, ships dock side by side, sometimes 4 or 5 ships deep, and in rows. On some occasions I had four ships to walk through to get onto the bank. It meant my cabin often had another ship a few feet away, making it dark. Venues like the dining room and lounge would have the same too. This, of course, happens on European river cruises too, but a difference here created another problem. Unlike in Europe, there is no shore side power to plug into. In Europe, the river cruise ships mostly switch their engines off and use electricity when docked. In Egypt the noisy and fume generating petrol engines run 24 hours a day, seven days a week whether they are sailing or not. And because they dock in rows close in front, I discovered that a cabin like mine close to the front is unbelievably noisy from those engines. That’s why the first and last night docked overnight in Luxor, even with earplugs, I ended up making a bed in my bathroom to close the door and reduce the noise. It was unbelievably noisy, so make sure you book midship as cabins at the rear could have the vibration and noise from your ship’s engines too. This is very key as ships are docked almost every night. We were docked for 6 of the 7 nights of the cruise! Not surprising with all the ships, the land-based visitors, and the limited sites to go to meant all places we went were extremely busy. The worst was the Valley of the Kings, which Nermeen, our Egyptologist, called the “Valley of the Tourists”. In the tombs it meant shuffling through with lines of people. Although other places including Queen Hatshepsut temple, Karnak and Luxor Temples in Luxor and in Cairo the pyramids and museum, were jam packed. One good thing about AmaWaterways and doing a seven-night itinerary is they did try visit when places when it would be less frantic. Like the Temple of Horus, we got there mid-morning when the bulk of the ships had finished their morning visits and were leaving, we called into Kom Ombo early evening and were the only ship there, and we went through Esna locks back to Luxor early morning when no other ships were. One of the downsides of my Nile River cruise was the vendors and the focus on shopping. It was a bit draining! Every site requires visitors to go through a market stall area to get in and out. There is no way round running the vendor gauntlet. The vendors are pushy, get in your face, and are persistent. So, I found I had to just get ready, walk briskly through, keep my eyes forward, not glance or look at anything, not let anyone put anything on me, nor take anything offered and keep constantly shaking my head saying, "No, no, no, no, no” (or “la, la, la” which means no). I was not that excited by what was on sale either. All seemed to be selling the same stuff, and looked mass-produced, and very touristy. On the upside, there did not seem to be other issues like pickpocketing, scams, and tricks other than over charging. Nermeen told us to always aim to pay only about 30% of what they start asking for. The other thing which I was less keen on, although we didn't have a lot on our trip were shopping trips dressed up as excursions. For example, we went to the Luxor Papyrus Institute, which really was a shopping trip. Though we did get a short demonstration on how papyrus is made and to paint our name in hieroglyphics on a prepared parchment in return. One of the things that I did constant think about during the trip was getting the upset stomach many warned about, and the impact it would have on going out for long trips to the sites knowing the toilets were limited and not generally appealing. I found the toilets overall at the sites were rather grubby and required paying to use. They did not cost much to use though, and one US dollar or less would suffice. But I would not want to do sit down in most! Unlike as number of people on the trip I did not get one. To avoid getting one, I used hand gel all the time when out, including after touching bank notes as that was a tip someone gave me. I used bottled water supplied by the line both on and off the ship, including when brushing my teeth and rinsing my toothbrush. I never had ice. I avoided salad, it's the first time in my life that I've gone for nearly two weeks eating no salad. And I only had fruit if it was peeled and only had cooked vegetables. I noticed all the bottles of water had reminders from the manufacturers to check it’s sealed before drinking, as vendors fill up empty bottles and sell them at the sites which is how some tourists get upset stomachs. People that did get upset stomachs told me they found Imodium, which is what I had brought with me, did not really work but a local remedy the ship provided for free if asked worked. So, that’s a tip to remember! The closer it got to going the issue of safety and security worried me as so many people raised it, and even the UK Foreign Office travel advice was full of watch outs. That sense of worry did hover during the trip, as everywhere we went there were armed military and police with barriers, checkpoints, and metal screenings to enter every site, the hotel, and up to three at airports. We also had police escorts a few times, like from the airport and when visiting Qena. And there was a lingering concern that I would invertedly do something wrong. For example, Walaa (the Cruise Manager) warned us to never take photos of the police or check points, be careful taking photos at airports as many have military aircraft too, not to take binoculars out as that could be misinterpreted as spying or suspicious, and he advised me to not take some of my camera equipment like tripods out that could cause some questions with the authorities. So, while that safety was a concern, I found travelling in the AmaWaterways “bubble” alleviated that as we always had someone with us to shepherd us around the issues, brief us on what to do and intervene if anything arose. There is much online about how LGBT people are harassed in Egypt but being in that bubble also helped I feel with the LGBT couples on our trip avoid being exposed to any of that risk. So, while security and safety were a concern, travelling in the AmaWaterways bubble for me made me feel comfortable and reassured. That of course for some will be a downside as it means staying within the bubble and not truly roaming, exploring, and engaging with the locals. I never left the bubble on the trip, although we did not really have time nor opportunity to, and I did have an amazing time which if I had chosen a cabin more midships would have edged it up even more. To get the answers to the most-asked questions I keep getting, including what the ships are really like, who should not go, coping with the heat, and the key question you really should be asking, watch this video. See you over there.

These massive and jaw-dropping temples were moved 62 metres up and 200 metres along to avoid the rising waters when Lake Nasser was built in the 1960s

videos.title: Cairo Travel Guide: 18 BEST Things to do in Cairo, Egypt
videos.description:
How to find cheap flights and travel more: https://travelforalmostfree.com/ref=beforeyougo2 // Here are the best things to do in Cairo, Egypt.

-------------------
Recommended Tours in Cairo:

Private Tour to Giza Pyramids and The Egyptian Museum: https://www.viator.com/tours/Cairo/Tour-to-Pyramids-and-The-Egyptian-Museum/d782-106218P1?pid=P00104625&mcid=42383&medium=link&medium_version=selector&campaign=yt-cairo-desc

Private tour to MEMPHIS CITY AND SAKKARA PYRAMID: https://www.viator.com/tours/Cairo/Full-day-tour-to-GIZA-PYRAMIDS-MEMPHIS-CITY-AND-SAKKARA-PYRAMID/d782-10726P49?pid=P00104625&mcid=42383&medium=link&medium_version=selector&campaign=yt-cairo-desc

Day Trip To Luxor From Cairo By Plane: https://www.viator.com/tours/Cairo/Day-trip-to-Luxor-from-Cairo-by-Plane-with-Lunch/d782-32214P86?pid=P00104625&mcid=42383&medium=link&medium_version=selector&campaign=yt-cairo-desc

2-Days Tour of the Unique White Desert from Cairo: https://www.viator.com/tours/Cairo/Enjoy-The-Unique-White-Desert-tour/d782-265673P2?pid=P00104625&mcid=42383&medium=link&medium_version=selector&campaign=yt-cairo-desc
-------------------

Welcome to our video on the top things to do in Cairo, the vibrant capital city of Egypt. Cairo is a city full of history and culture, offering visitors a range of experiences, from exploring ancient landmarks to enjoying modern shopping and dining.
 
First on our list is the Great Pyramids of Giza, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. These iconic pyramids are over 4,500 years old and are a testament to the engineering and architectural prowess of the ancient Egyptians.
 
Next up is the Egyptian Museum, home to one of the world's largest collections of ancient Egyptian artifacts. The museum houses over 120,000 items, including mummies, sculptures, and precious artifacts from King Tutankhamun's tomb.
 
For a taste of local culture, head to the Khan El-Khalili bazaar, one of the oldest and largest bazaars in the Middle East. This bustling market is a labyrinth of alleys and stalls selling everything from spices and textiles to jewelry and souvenirs.
 
Another must-see historical site is the Saladin Citadel, a fortress built in the 12th century and located on a hill overlooking Cairo. The citadel has a museum inside, showcasing the history of Cairo and the Muslim world.
 
For those interested in nature, take a stroll through the Al-Azhar Park, a lush green oasis in the middle of the city. The park features walking trails, picnic areas, and a range of recreational activities, as well as stunning views of the city skyline.
 
For a taste of modern Cairo, head to the Cairo Festival City Mall. This expansive shopping center features a range of international and local brands, as well as a range of restaurants and cafes.
 
Last but not least, don't miss the chance to experience the local food scene. Cairo is known for its delicious and diverse cuisine, from traditional Egyptian dishes like koshari and fuul to international cuisine. Be sure to try some local favorites like konafa and baladi bread.
 
There you have it, our top picks for the best things to do in Cairo. Whether you're interested in history, culture, nature, or food, this city has something for everyone. So what are you waiting for? Start planning your trip today and discover all that Cairo has to offer.
 
Keep watching to see the top things to do in the Cairo, top attractions in Cairo, places to visit in the Cairo and Middle East travel guides.

Subscribe for travel inspiration videos and travel guides! Inspired by Touropia, expedia, Wolters World, Beautiful Destinations, MojoTravels & Tourradar.

tags: things to do in cairo egypt
videos.transcript: as the home of one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and countless other mesmerizing attractions Cairo offers an unforgettable experience for every kind of traveler here are the top things to do in Cairo Egypt number 18. Cairo Citadel Cairo Citadel an impressive medieval Islamic fortification offers you a fantastic glimpse into Egypt's Rich History built by the famous solid din in the 12th century this historical landmark showcases stunning examples of Islamic architecture as well as breathtaking Hilltop views of the old city within the fast complex you can explore various museums and religious sites that highlight aspects of Egyptian history and diverse Artistic Styles a mandatory attraction within Cairo Citadel is the mosque of Muhammad Ali an Exquisite Showcase of Ottoman Empire design as you wander through its Grand Halls adorned with intricate details and vibrant colors or Marvel at its towering minarets from outside it becomes clear why this mosque remains one of Egypt's most beloved tourist attractions number 17 hanging Church the hanging Church an iconic Gem of Coptic Christianity and one of Egypt's oldest churches is an outstanding historical site when exploring Cairo believed to have been built in 690 A.D this captivating basilican-style Church holds great cultural significance and has been the site for numerous apparitions of Mary most notably during the miracle of makato mountain located in the heart of old Cairo this remarkable architecture includes a stunning 19th century facade boasting Twin Towers along with intricate biblical frescoes lining the interior walls its historical and cultural importance makes it an unmissable part of any traveler's itinerary who wishes to delve deep into Egypt's Rich history Beyond just pyramids and museums number 16 Islamic Cairo delve into the heart of cairo's history and immerse yourself in the rich cultural heritage found with in Islamic Cairo this historic core established long before modern European architecture filled downtown Cairo holds one of the largest collections of Islamic architectural masterpieces in the world take a DIY walking tour along mui Street to uncover hidden gems like restored ottoman era homes and quaint coffee shops where locals gather over steaming Cups of Tea for an unforgettable experience venture out for some nighttime exploration witness Majestic landmarks cast under soft lighting as Lively markets Buzz with energy around you number 15. al-azhar park al-azhar park is a remarkable destination for travelers visiting Cairo this public park offers much needed recreational space for the city's over 15 million residents and is surrounded by some of the most significant historic districts of Islamic Cairo the park features Heavenly Gardens that provide visitors with a tranquil escape from the hustle and bustle of the city while enjoying encompassing views of old minarets throughout old Islamic Cairo number 14. all Azar mosque located near al-azhar Park all Azar mosque is one of the most significant historical sites to visit when in Cairo this iconic Landmark has stood for over a thousand years and boasts an intricate architectural style that blends old and modern designs built by the fatimids in 1970 A.D it was initially formed as a Center for Learning as well as worship nowadays it's an important educational institution that offers courses on various subjects including quranic interpretation Hadith studies Arabic language law and jurisprudence what sets al-azhar mosque apart from other mosques is its importance to Muslim history and its role in facilitating spiritual pilgrimage it houses some of the most important Scholars globally which makes it one of the leading educational institutions worldwide today number 13 Coptic Cairo Coptic Cairo is an essential neighborhood in Cairo for anyone interested in early Christianity and religious pilgrimage this historic area contains some of Egypt's most Splendid churches dating from the time between ancient Egypt and the Islamic period one of the highlights of Coptic Cairo is Saint sergius and Bacchus church or Abu sarga believed to be built on the spot where Mary Joseph and Jesus Christ rested during their escape to Egypt don't miss out on visiting the Coptic Museum while you're there it houses about 20 000 artifacts from the Coptic period making it one of the largest museums with ancient Christian monuments number 12 Khan al-kalili located in the Heart of the City this famous bizarre and souk has been a symbol of trade for over 600 years as you explore through its winding alleys you'll come across cultural structures that date back to Medieval Times Khan Al khalili is not only known for its antique shops but also its Spice Market and perfume vendors additionally if you're looking for high quality Fabrics or handmade car carpet to take home this is the place to go with so much history etched in every corner it's no wonder why many people say this is the most instagramable place in Cairo number 11. Museum of Islamic Art the Museum of Islamic Art in Cairo is a must-see for art and history enthusiasts its vast collection of rare woodwork artwork and artifacts from the Islamic world is simply exceptional with nearly 18 fascinating exhibits housed in historic Cairo this Museum showcases magnificent masterpieces including architectural forms and ornamentation from monuments and mosques from mamluk glass to turkmen carpets in Fatimid Stone workmanship there are breathtaking collections dating back to pre-mamluk times through various dynasties up until Ottoman rules centuries later a visit to this remarkable museum is an essential part of any trip to Egypt number 10 garbage City nestled behind the makotom hills there is a village in Cairo called garbage City although it might sound unappealing to visit this community is an extraordinary example of recycling and waste management practices the zabalin live and work here collecting garbage from all over Cairo and sorting it for recycling almost nothing goes to waste as they recycle about 85 percent of everything collected despite its reputation garbage city has become a popular tourist destination where visitors can learn about environmental sustainability while interacting with the locals to support Community Development and social justice causes ready to explore the world don't miss A Beat hit that subscribe button so you can be the first to know when we release new travel guides and inspiration number nine cicada and dashur sakata and dashur are two of the most fascinating historical sites to visit near Cairo sakata is home to the oldest complete stone structures in Egypt including the iconic step pyramid of Jose this ancient site contains numerous tombs temples and pyramids that provide a glimpse into ancient Egyptian civilization the Shore's Royal necropolis is located 25 miles south of Cairo and houses several of the largest oldest and most impressive pyramids in all of Egypt the bent pyramid and Red Pyramid are just two examples of this incredible funerary architecture that visitors can explore on location number 8. Manila Palace located on the Niles Eastern Bank this unique and stunning Palace Museum was once home to Prince Muhammad Ali a member of Egypt's royal family this public art and history museum features beautifully landscaped Gardens and forested nature parks filled with intricate mosaics and fountains the opulent Manila Palace showcases an impressive interior design with ornate details from around the world including Persian carpets Spanish tiles Indian woodwork Turkish bath houses antique furniture and much more packed within its walls number 7. Memphis ancient city as a UNESCO world world heritage site and the ancient capital of Egypt Memphis holds significant historical importance located just 15 miles south of Cairo on the West Bank of the Nile River Memphis houses many necropolises where you can explore great monuments that are important parts of ancient Egyptian culture you'll get to see the 33 feet long lying statue of Ramses II the Colossal Limestone Phoenix and the fascinating Museum that houses many historical Treasures you can also explore the well-preserved ruins of the ancient city and immerse yourself in the history and culture of ancient Egypt number six baharia Oasis a great day trip from Cairo baharia Oasis is a Hidden Gem in the middle of the Egyptian desert that is waiting to be discovered this gorgeous depression allows you to experience first hand how ancient Egyptians found respite from their daily troubles with its Crystal Clear Hot Springs towering sand dunes and picturesque Palm Groves bahadia Oasis is a breathtaking side to behold you can explore the nearby black and white deserts visit ancient tombs and temples and even go on a thrilling desert Safari number 5. cave Church the cave Church also known as Saint Simon the Tanner Monastery is a peculiar historical and religious monument in Cairo it's one of the largest churches not only within Egypt but across the Middle East this iconic Church offers a tranquil space for meditation and reflection with its architectural wonder which includes several caves built into separate Church spaces the cave church has become a spiritual destination for many Travelers due to its unique architecture and Serene surroundings interestingly enough it is said that the Holy Family stayed in a cave beneath this site during their journey into Egypt number four Egyptian museum a trip to Cairo would not be complete without a visit to the world famous Egyptian Museum located in tarir Square Downtown Cairo this Museum houses over 120 thousand and artifacts from ancient Egypt and is home to the largest collection of pharaohonic Antiquities in the world you will be fascinated by the exhibits showcasing pharaohonic history including King Tutankhamun's Treasures hieroglyphics mummies and other archaeological Treasures the Egyptian Museum offers Travelers a glimpse into Egypt's rich and fascinating culture and serves as a Gateway into understanding their unique Nile river civilization it is one of the most visited tourist attractions in Cairo with many tours operating daily to Showcase its amazing collections number three obdeen Palace Museum obdean Palace Museum is a true Testament to the opulence and Grandeur of Egyptian royal families from the outside the palace's architecture is a stunning blend of European and Islamic styles with intricate carvings detailed mosaics and Grand arches inside the palace is even more impressive with grand Halls sweeping staircases and beautifully decorated rooms each room boasts its own unique design with Rich Fabrics or Nate furnishings and exquisite artwork adorning the walls the Museum's collection is equally impressive with a vast array of ART furniture and artifacts that offer a fascinating look into the lives of Egypt's royal families from antique clocks to intricate Ceramics The Collection is a testament to the country's Rich cultural heritage number 2. Nile River the Nile River is an essential part of Egyptian history and culture and a visit to Cairo is incomplete without exploring this vital Waterway you can take a cruise along the Nile River or explore its banks on foot to experience Egypt's Rich cultural heritage besides being Egypt's primary source of water and food production the Nile also played a crucial role in ancient transportation and trade routes number one the Pyramids of Giza one cannot visit Egypt without seeing the iconic Pyramids of Giza these ancient structures are among the world's most famous landmarks attracting visitors from all over the globe built over 4 500 years ago they were initially constructed as tombs for Pharaohs and their consorts beside the pyramid lies one of Egypt's most recognizable statues the Great Sphinx believed to have been crafted during Pharaoh coffrey's reign in around 2500 BC this immense Limestone statue has a lion body with a human head believed to be representing King coffrey himself or Guardian deity of his funerary complex click here for more things to do in Egypt foreign

safetyNotes: Last but not least, don't miss the chance to experience the local food scene

videos.title: Best Things To Do in Cairo Egypt 2026 4K
videos.description:
Are you looking for the best things to do in Cairo? We just created a list of 20 must do activities for you to do while visiting Cairo Egypt! In this Cairo travel guide we show you around the Nile River, pyramids of Giza, Alexandria, the Red Sea, Mt Sinai and many other top tourist destinations in Cairo.  Some of those Cairo attractions include Khan al Khalili bazar, New Cairo City, Sphinx, Egyptian food, Siwa Oasis, Coptic Church and much more!  If you enjoy this Cairo ravel video you can also watch our other travel vlogs from across Egypt.

It took 5 days to make this Cairo travel video. I would say you can explore most of Cairo in  in 3 to 5 days but if you want to see do day trips to Red Sea, Mt Sinai, Alexandria then consider 7 to 10 days. Mostly because Bali traffic can be quite dense. You can easily do these attractions on your ownI do recommend a local guide for first time visitors. Make sure you find a registered tour guide and company with good reviews in Cairo You can find the best Cairo hotels along the Nile or in New Cairo City. The best food in Cairo is available in hotel restaurants or on the night cruises on the Nile from my experience.

videos.transcript: [Music] planning your first trip to Cairo you're in for a treat Cairo is the largest in the Arab world and the Middle East it covers a massive area stretching 120 km from the Gulf of Suez to the Suez Canal and 165 km south of the Mediterranean with a city this vast there's no shortage of incredible sights and experiences waiting for you so get ready because I'm about to share all the must knows for making the most of your Cairo Adventure plus stick around until the end of the video for more Insider tips to help you navigate the local food scene like a pro at number five let's talk about how to dress right in Cairo there are two things you need to keep in mind when packing the weather and the culture Egypt is a predominantly Muslim country so modest dressing is important especially when you're exploring non-touristy spots or visiting religious sites for those places make sure to cover your shoulders and knees ladies you'll also want to pack a lightweight scarf for covering your hair when you visit mosques now you're probably wondering how do you stay cool when you need to be covered up Egypt's climate is hot and dry with May to October being the hottest months and having the most intense Sunshine here's the trick wear breathable clothing think of fabrics like linen and cotton to keep you cool while still being respectful loose long sleeve tops wide leg pants and maxi skirts are great options for coverage without sacrificing comfort and don't forget the accessories sun hats and light scarves are Lifesavers for protecting your head and neck from the Sun Plus Pack a good pair of sunglasses apply lots of sunscreen and bring some sturdy sandals or comfy walking shoes to keep you ready for whatever Cairo throws your way at number four let's dive into getting around Cairo focusing on the best ways to see the sights while driving is technically an opt it's not one I'd recommend traffic can be pretty chaotic and not everyone follows the road rules to save yourself the stress consider hiring a private driver and guide instead you can book a car with a professional driver through thirdparty sites like Viet tour for about $100 or 4,878 Egyptian pounds per day for your group and if you only need a one-way transfer it's even more budget friendly if that's a bit over your price range or if you want to experience the city like a local public transportation is another option just keep in mind that buses and trains tend to be crowded hot and not always the cleanest plus they're slower than private cars since they make plenty of stops despite that the fairs are pretty cheap expect to pay around 7 Egyptian pounds for a regular bus and 13 Egyptian pounds for an air conditioned one taxi start at a base Fair of 8.5 Egyptian pounds with an extra for Egyptian pounds per kilometer after that both private and public options have their perks so the choice is ultimately up to you coming in at number three we have hotel accommodations for firsttime travelers I always suggest putting comfort and convenience over anything else so don't be afraid to splurge on top-notch hotels at Marriott Mina house Cairo the Pyramids of Giza feel almost within reach this top luxury hotel was originally built in 1869 as a hunting lodge for Egypt's kiv Ismael Pasha and has since become a historic gem hosting politicians Royals and dignitaries it's even iconic enough to earn a mention in Agatha Christie's death on the Nile though the rooms have been updated with modern Comforts The more premium options offer breathtaking views of the Great Pyramid making it an unforgettable spot to enjoy your morning coffee the Cairo Marriott Hotel boasts a grand history that adds to its unique appeal originally built in 1869 as a royal residence to host French emperor Napoleon III during the Suez Canal's grand opening it was modeled after the Palace of Versailles to exude that same level of Elegance by the early 1900s the property transformed into a luxury hotel seamlessly blending its historic charm with modern touches now most rooms are housed in two contemporary Towers but the heart of the hotel remains true to its roots as a stunning five-star Boutique Hotel in the heart of old Cairo Larry odd Hotel Des Sharm offers a perfect blend of history and luxury that's just a short stroll from the bustling con El Khal Bazaar inspired by the ottoman style architecture of bate El sahmi the hotel's Interiors transport you back in time while each of the 17 themed Suites showcases a unique aspect of kiro's architectural charm the rooms come alive with colorful fomed textiles and captivating artwork by local artists for dining the rooftop restaurant serves up delicious home-cooked Middle Eastern dishes with a side of City views and the Charming tea Lounge offers a variety of breakfast lunch and dinner options to keep you fueled for your Cairo Adventures there's no shortage of beautiful accommodations in the city and the best part is a lot of them are great value for your money the average cost of five-star hotels in Cairo is about $131 or about 6,400 Egyptian pounds with some median hotels ranging anywhere from $0 to $70 a night at number two let's take a closer look at their local Customs starting with their concept of time here time is famously flexible so be prepared for things to get delayed or change at the last minute it's all part of the experience as much as possible keep your plans fluid and try to go with the flow in case any surprises come up now let's talk shopping because haggling is a big part of the culture if you're planning to bargain here's how to do it like a pro first don't buy from the first shop you visit ask around and and compare prices from at least three different sellers to get a sense of the average cost second keep a poker face don't let on that you're excited or sellers might raise the price and third even if you're happy with a price ask if it's their final offer if they don't budge mention you found a cheaper one elsewhere and see if they reconsider when meeting locals always start with assalamu Alum or peace be upon you it's a common greeting that goes a long way in showing respect ipping or bakish is deeply embedded in cairo's culture for small services like watching over your shoes at a mosque tips usually range from 1 to 2 Egyptian pounds while opening a door or letting you climb a minet might cost around 5 or 10 Egyptian pounds at restaurants tipping varies typically from 3 to 25 Egyptian pounds depending on the level of service there's also a cultural aspect to bakish that's tied to almsgiving for Egyptians helping those in need isn't just a kind Gest it's considered a moral duty and a fundamental part of Islam giving money or Goods to the less fortunate especially the disabled is a common practice so visitors are encouraged to participate by giving small change as it's a meaningful way to connect with the local culture and contribute to the community before we dive into the top spot I've got some Insider tips for finding the best spots to enjoy local flavors although Egyptian street food is super affordable it can come with with a side of tummy troubles to avoid that check out places known for their hygiene and still get that authentic Taste elid Restaurant is perfect for trying circassian chicken and walnut sauce or even the classic Egyptian specialty pigeon and bonus you can find this restaurant all over the city for the Ultimate kosari Experience head to Abu Tark this place is practically legendary and even got Anthony Bourdain's stamp of approval want to twist on street food zerba serves all the Egyptian favorites but in a stylish colorful setting that makes every bite feel a bit more special don't miss out on local cafes either Eli is famous for Rose be Laban or Egyptian rice pudding while granita serves up an unforgettable toffee date cake that's a must try now that you know the secret to where you can find the best authentic dishes let's unveil what's at the top spot coming in at number one I'm sharing the best spots every firsttime traveler must see in Cairo the Egyptian Museum is an absolute treasure chest of ancient artifacts with over 120,000 items on display including the world famous burial Treasures of Tuten Kon it's one of the most iconic museums in the world but here's a tip it gets packed especially when the tour groups arrive so getting their early is the key to beating the crowds tier Square isn't just a historic site it's a living symbol of Egypt's Modern Day history it's been at the center of many protests and uprisings that shaped the country including the 2011 reol Evolution Islamic Cairo a UNESCO world heritage site that was founded in the 10th century is a mustsee for its Rich history it features the Salah Aldine Citadel which served as the administrative hub for Egyptian rulers for over 700 years the entrance fee is 60 Egyptian pounds for adults and 30 Egyptian pounds for students and while you're there explore the famous Khan El Khal Bazaar where you can shop for Unique souvenirs and intriguing trinkets then just outside Cairo the Giza necropolis awaits it's home to the iconic pyramids of kufu kafra and Menor plus it also includes smaller pyramids temples cemeteries a worker's Village and of course the Great Sphinx of Giza it's the ultimate videos.title: Expat Guide to Egypt: Best Neighborhoods in Cairo for Expats
videos.description:
In this video, I break down the best Cairo neighborhoods to live in as an expat depending on your needs and interests. Egypt can be overwhelming to even the most seasoned traveler, and figuring out where to live in Cairo can feel like looking for a pebble in a ballpit. Each expat moving to Cairo has his or her own concerns, priorities, and needs, so in this video I’ve addressed some of the bigger issues facing expats and recommended neighborhoods in Cairo accordingly. For some, downtown Cairo may be perfect, but others may find comfort in the Fifth Settlement or 6th of October City. I hope the video brings some clarity to Cairo’s urban layout, and let me know if you have any questions in the comments.

I started a new channel about traveling and living in Egypt: subscribe at @RashadTravelsandStuff 

Subscribe to the channel and like the video for more Egypt-related content.


Names of mentioned neighborhoods:

Tagamoa el-Khames/Fifth Settlement = التجمع الخامس
Madinaty = مدينتي
Sheikh Zayed City = مدينة الشيغ زايد
6th of October = متة اكتوبر

Downtown Cairo = وسط البلد
Mohandiseen = مهندسين
Dokki/Doqi = الدقي
Agouza = العجوزة

Garden City = جاردن سيتي
Zamalek = زمالك
Maadi = المعادي

00:00 Intro
00:51 Traffic
01:33 Desert Suburbs
04:10 Upscale Urban Areas
06:57 Middle-Class Urban Areas
09:24 Poorer Neighborhoods

be lots of restaurants with menus entirely in English a lot of English-speaking people will be there and it's actually home to an American Style Diner and a Mexican restaurant called Gringos believe it or not I do not recommend Gringo sucks my idea is also green this is a big point of emphasis because there are not a ton of places in Cairo that have much Greenery so this could be a really great Best of Both Worlds a quiet and tranquil neighborhood where you can walk with your kids on the street walk with them to school it's almost like the Yuppie gentrified parts of Brooklyn our last category Urban middle class areas these areas are perfect for people who want to really immerse themselves in cairo's culture and energy for many expats a work transfer is the thing that motivates their move to Egypt but for some Cairo in and of itself is the ultimate destination because it's just that it is the capital of Egypt the cultural capital of the Arab world they come to learn they come to walk its streets to connect with its inhabitants and to be amongst the energy and excitement that make Cairo one of the most dynamic and unique places in the world personally I for sure Fell in This category when I first moved to Egypt trust me if you're looking to immerse yourself in the city and the culture look for downtown agusa mohandasin and ducky I've put the names again to all these places in the description these areas are centrally located downtown is literally the center of the city and they let you live a pretty regular Egyptian lifestyle and at the same time they have relatively good accommodations at a reasonable price point downtown has a lot of bars and its cafes are some of the most iconic in all of Egypt also these are the cafes that women will feel probably the most comfortable in generally downtown also has some of cairo's most iconic restaurants Abu thought it for kosheri Pizzazz for Shawarma and so so so many others you can really never run out of things to do just in downtown and you'll often find yourself meeting friends in downtown so if you've already lived there you can save yourself the cost of an Uber or a horribly long time in traffic mohandasin and ducky they allow you to live in a pretty normal Egyptian Community popular cafes markets if you want to buy your produce outside of a grocery store and restaurants that are not really expensive in bougie these areas would be perfect for somebody who said as they don't want to live quite like a foreigner within this category I personally lived in mohandasin I went to work I played tennis at a local club that was maybe a two-minute walk from where I lived this club had absolutely no expats it was just Egyptian families I went to the market in the area of KitKat and met up mostly with my friends at a cafe where you sit outside and they're massive flat screen TVs and you play FIFA until as late as you want and when I say late I mean literally as late as you want I was living how a lot of Egyptian guys my age lived and to me that felt pretty cool there may be some of you out there who really want to live in the most quote-unquote Egyptian Place possible really rough it to get the most authentic experience you can possibly get I do not recommend living in these areas places like Islamic Cairo imbaba Hussein bullet all of these areas have so much going on and are really really interesting places but apartments are almost never rented to foreigners not to mention potential problems with utilities internet access and general quality of life differences that most westerners are just not used to enduring unless you are on a mission to live in a very poor area for some reason I would advise completely against it so to recap we have our desert suburbs our upscale Urban neighborhoods and our middle class Urban neighborhoods if you want space and Western amenities head to the desert if you want to be in the city but you want to be super comfortable and you have some money to throw around look to Zamalek Garden City and mayady and for a lifestyle closer to that of a typical Egyptian look to downtown or hostel Belair as it's called mohandasin and ducky if you're still here thanks for watching I hope you found it very helpful like this video and throw me a subscribe for more Middle East and specifically Egypt related content keep exploring have fun in Cairo and hope to see you soon [Music] I'm going out
